revision.text: #redirect[[en:Suijin_Bridge]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]

[[画像:Midukamioohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|東京都墨田区から。(2005年8月)]] '''水神大橋'''（すいじんおおはし）は、[[隅田川]]にかかる[[橋]]。西岸は[[荒川区]]南千住八丁目、東岸は[[墨田区]]堤通二丁目。橋名は東岸にある「[[隅田川神社]]（水神宮）」に因む。
創架は[[1989年]]（[[平成]]元年）3月。付近の[[汐入公園]]、[[東白鬚公園]]は東京都の防災拠点として位置づけられており、そのため双方を繋ぐ連絡橋として[[千住汐入大橋]]と供に計画された。当初は歩行者専用橋で、接続する道路の整備が完了した平成8年（[[1996年]]）7月10日より自動車道路共用が開始された。
もともと「[[水神の渡し]]」という渡船場があった場所でもある。
==橋の概要== *構造形式 3径間鋼ニールセンローゼ[[桁橋]] *橋長 157.0ｍ（最大支間長 102.0m） *幅員 17.0ｍ *着工 [[昭和]]59年 *竣工 [[平成]]元年3月 *施工主体 東京都 *橋梁設計 東京エンジニアリング *橋桁製作 [[三菱重工業]]
==隣の橋== （上流）－[[千住大橋]]－[[千住汐入大橋]]－''水神大橋''－[[白鬚橋]]－[[桜橋]]－（下流）
[[Category:日本の橋|すいしんおおはし]]

[[画像:Midukamioohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|東京都墨田区から。(2005年8月)]] '''水神大橋'''（すいじんおおはし）は、[[隅田川]]にかかる[[東京都道461号吾妻橋伊興町線]]支線の[[橋]]。西岸は[[荒川区]][[南千住 (荒川区)|南千住]]八丁目、東岸は[[墨田区]]堤通二丁目。橋名は東岸にある「[[隅田川神社]]（水神宮）」に因む。
創架は[[1989年]]（[[平成]]元年）3月。橋の組み立てを陸上で行い、橋桁を台船に乗せて干満の差を利用して橋脚に乗せるという方法を利用した。
付近の[[汐入公園]]、[[東白鬚公園]]は東京都の防災拠点として位置づけられており、そのため双方を繋ぐ連絡橋として[[千住汐入大橋]]と共に計画された。当初は歩行者専用橋で、接続する道路の整備が完了した平成8年（[[1996年]]）7月10日より自動車道路供用が開始された。
もともと「[[隅田川の渡し|水神の渡し]]」という渡船場があった場所でもある。
==橋の概要== *種別 - 鋼道路橋 *形式 - 3径間連続[[アーチ橋#補剛桁を有するアーチ|ニールセンローゼ桁橋]] *橋長 - 157.0ｍ *最大支間 - 102.0m *幅員 - 17.0ｍ *着工 - [[昭和]]59年（1984年） *竣工 - [[平成]]元年（1989年）3月 *事業主体 - 東京都 *橋梁設計 - 東京エンジニアリング *橋桁製作 - [[三菱重工業]]
==隣の橋== （上流）－[[千住大橋]]－[[千住汐入大橋]]－'''水神大橋'''－[[白鬚橋]]－[[桜橋 (東京都)|桜橋]]－（下流）

==隣の橋== （上流）－[[千住大橋]]－[[千住汐入大橋]]－'''水神大橋'''－[[白鬚橋]]－[[桜橋 (東京都)|桜橋]]－（下流）

{{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=東京|number=461|align=left}} [[ファイル:Suijin ohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|東京都墨田区から(2008年1月)]] [[ファイル:Midukamioohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|東京都墨田区から(2005年8月)]] '''水神大橋'''（すいじんおおはし）は、[[隅田川]]にかかる[[東京都道461号吾妻橋伊興町線]]支線の[[橋]]。西岸は[[荒川区]][[南千住]]八丁目、東岸は[[墨田区]]堤通二丁目。橋名は東岸にある「[[隅田川神社]]（水神宮）」に因む。
創架は[[1989年]]（[[平成]]元年）3月。橋の組み立てを陸上で行い、橋桁を台船に乗せて干満の差を利用して橋脚に乗せるという方法を利用した。
付近の[[汐入公園]]、[[東白鬚公園]]は東京都の防災拠点として位置づけられており、そのため双方を繋ぐ連絡橋として[[千住汐入大橋]]と共に計画された。当初は歩行者専用橋で、接続する道路の整備が完了した平成8年（[[1996年]]）7月10日より自動車道路供用が開始された。
もともと「[[隅田川の渡し|水神の渡し]]」という渡船場があった場所でもある。
== 橋の概要 == *種別 - 鋼道路橋 *形式 - 3径間連続[[アーチ橋#補剛桁を有するアーチ|ニールセンローゼ桁橋]] *橋長 - 157.0m *最大支間 - 102.0m *幅員 - 17.0m *着工 - [[昭和]]59年（1984年） *竣工 - [[平成]]元年（1989年）3月 *事業主体 - 東京都 *橋梁設計 - 東京エンジニアリング *橋桁製作 - [[三菱重工業]]

(2016年5月)]] [[ファイル:100 views edo 037.jpg|サムネイル|200px|水神の渡し]] '''水神大橋'''（すいじんおおはし）は、[[隅田川]]にかかる[[東京都道461号吾妻橋伊興町線]]支線の[[橋]]。西岸は[[荒川区]][[南千住]]八丁目、東岸は[[墨田区]]堤通二丁目。橋名は東岸にある「[[隅田川神社]]（水神宮）」に因む。 創架は[[1989年]]（[[平成]]元年）3月。橋の組み立てを陸上で行い、橋桁を台船に乗せて干満の差を利用して橋脚に乗せるという方法を利用した。 付近の[[汐入公園]]、[[東白鬚公園]]は東京都の防災拠点として位置づけられており、そのため双方を繋ぐ連絡橋として[[千住汐入大橋]]と共に計画された。当初は歩行者専用橋で、接続する道路の整備が完了した平成8年（[[1996年]]）7月10日より自動車道路供用が開始された。 もともと「[[隅田川の渡し|水神の渡し]]」という渡船場があった場所でもある。 == 橋の概要 == *種別 - 鋼道路橋 *形式 - 3径間連続[[アーチ橋#補剛桁を有するアーチ|ニールセンローゼ桁橋]] *橋長 - 157.0m *最大支間 - 102.0m *幅員 - 17.0m *着工 - [[昭和]]59年（1984年） *竣工 - [[平成]]元年（1989年）3月 *事業主体 - 東京都 *橋梁設計 - 東京エンジニアリング *橋桁製作 - [[三菱重工業]] == 路線バス == * [[都営バス]] ** [[都営バス南千住営業所#里22・南千47・南千48系統|南千48]] [[南千住駅]]東口 - 墨田二丁目 - 白鬚橋 - 東向島広小路 - [[亀戸駅]]（昼間のみ） == 隣の橋 == * [[隅田川]] : （上流） - [[隅田川橋梁 (東京地下鉄日比谷線)|隅田川橋梁]] - [[千住汐入大橋]] - '''水神大橋''' - [[白鬚橋]] - [[桜橋 (東京都)|桜橋]] - （下流） {{隅田川の橋}} {{commonscat|Suijin Great Bridge}} {{Coord|35|44|10|N|139|48|51|E|display=title|region:JP_type:landmark}} {{DEFAULTSORT:すいしんおおはし}} [[Category:東京都区部の橋]] [[Category:荒川区の交通‎]] [[Category:南千住]] [[Category:墨田区の橋]] [[Category:隅田川の橋]]

{{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=東京|number=461|align=left}} [[ファイル:Suijin ohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|東京都墨田区から(2008年1月)]] [[ファイル:Suijin Ohashi - skytree - may 15 2016.jpg|thumb|200px|[[汐入公園]]から (2016年5月)]] [[ファイル:100 views edo 037.jpg|サムネイル|200px|水神の渡し]] '''水神大橋'''（すいじんおおはし）は、[[隅田川]]にかかる[[東京都道461号吾妻橋伊興町線]]支線の[[橋]]。西岸は[[荒川区]][[南千住]]八丁目、東岸は[[墨田区]]堤通二丁目。橋名は東岸にある「[[隅田川神社]]（水神宮）」に因む。
創架は[[1989年]]（[[平成]]元年）3月。橋の組み立てを陸上で行い、橋桁を台船に乗せて干満の差を利用して橋脚に乗せるという方法を利用した。
付近の[[汐入公園]]、[[東白鬚公園]]は東京都の防災拠点として位置づけられており、そのため双方を繋ぐ連絡橋として[[千住汐入大橋]]と共に計画された。当初は歩行者専用橋で、接続する道路の整備が完了した平成8年（[[1996年]]）7月10日より自動車道路供用が開始された。
もともと「[[隅田川の渡し|水神の渡し]]」という渡船場があった場所でもある。
== 橋の概要 == *種別 - 鋼道路橋 *形式 - 3径間連続[[アーチ橋#補剛桁を有するアーチ|ニールセンローゼ桁橋]] *橋長 - 157.0m *最大支間 - 102.0m *幅員 - 17.0m *着工 - [[昭和]]59年（1984年） *竣工 - [[平成]]元年（1989年）3月 *事業主体 - 東京都 *橋梁設計 - 東京エンジニアリング *橋桁製作 - [[三菱重工業]]

橋の東詰で、[[首都高速6号向島線]][[堤通出入口]]に接続する。
もともと「[[隅田川の渡し|水神の渡し]]」という渡船場があった場所でもある。

revision.comment: Created page with "{{Cite sources|date=July 2024}} {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=461|align=left}} [[File:Suijin ohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View from Sumida Ward, Tokyo (January 2008)]] [[File:Midukamioohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View of Suijin Ōhashi from the Shuto Expressway (August 2005)]] [[File:100 views edo 037.jpg|thumb|200px|Suijin no Watashi]] '''Suijin Ōhashi''' (水神大橋) is a bridge spanning the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川}}, carrying a spur of the {{ill|Tokyo Metropo..."
revision.text:
{{Cite sources|date=July 2024}} {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=461|align=left}} [[File:Suijin ohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View from Sumida Ward, Tokyo (January 2008)]] [[File:Midukamioohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View of Suijin Ōhashi from the Shuto Expressway (August 2005)]] [[File:100 views edo 037.jpg|thumb|200px|Suijin no Watashi]]
'''Suijin Ōhashi''' (水神大橋) is a bridge spanning the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川}}, carrying a spur of the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Route 461|ja|東京都道461号吾妻橋伊興町線}}. The western end of the bridge is located in {{ill|Minami-Senju|ja|南千住}} 8-chōme, Arakawa Ward, while the eastern end is in Tsuijidō 2-chōme, Sumida Ward. The bridge's name originates from the nearby {{ill|Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|ja|隅田川神社}}, also known as Suijin-gū.
The bridge was first constructed in March 1989 (Heisei 1). Its assembly was carried out on land, and the bridge girders were transported on barges to the site, where tidal changes were used to place them on the piers.
Nearby parks, {{ill|Shioiri Park|ja|汐入公園}} and {{ill|Higashi Shirahige Park|ja|東白鬚公園}}, serve as disaster response bases for Tokyo. As part of the disaster preparedness plan, Suijin Ōhashi was built alongside {{ill|Senju Shioiri Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋}} to connect these parks. Initially a pedestrian-only bridge, it was opened to vehicular traffic on July 10, 1996 (Heisei 8), following the completion of the connecting roads.
On its eastern side, Suijin Ōhashi connects to the {{ill|Shuto Expressway Mukōjima Line|ja|首都高速6号向島線}} at the {{ill|Tsuijidō Entrance|ja|堤通出入口}}.
This location was historically home to the ferry crossing "{{ill|Suijin no Watashi|ja|水神の渡し}}."
== Bridge Specifications == * **Type**: Steel road bridge * **Structure**: Three-span continuous Nielsen-Lohse girder arch bridge * **Length**: 157.0 meters * **Main span**: 102.0 meters * **Width**: 17.0 meters * **Construction started**: 1984 (Shōwa 59) * **Completed**: March 1989 (Heisei 1) * **Project owner**: Tokyo Metropolitan Government * **Design**: Tokyo Engineering * **Girder fabrication**: {{ill|Mitsubishi Heavy Industries|ja|三菱重工業}}
== Public Transportation == * **{{ill|Toei Bus|ja|都営バス}}** - {{ill|Route 40|ja|都営バス南千住営業所#錦40系統}}: {{ill|Minami-Senju Station|ja|南千住駅}} East Exit - Sumida 2-chōme - Shirahige Bridge - Higashi Mukōjima Hirokōji - {{ill|Tokyo Skytree Station|ja|とうきょうスカイツリー駅}} Front - {{ill|Kinshichō Station|ja|錦糸町駅}} Front
== Nearby Bridges == * {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川}} : (Upstream) - {{ill|Sumida River Bridge (Tokyo Metro Hibiya Line)|ja|隅田川橋梁 (東京地下鉄日比谷線)}} - {{ill|Senju Shioiri Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋}} - '''Suijin Ōhashi''' - {{ill|Shirahige Bridge|ja|白鬚橋}} - {{ill|Sakura Bridge (Tokyo)|ja|桜橋 (東京都)}} - (Downstream)
{{Sumida River Bridges}} {{commonscat|Suijin Great Bridge}} {{Coord|35|44|10|N|139|48|51|E|display=title|region:JP_type:landmark}}
[[Category:Bridges in Tokyo]] [[Category:Transport in Arakawa Ward]] [[Category:Minami-Senju]] [[Category:Bridges in Sumida Ward]] [[Category:Sumida River bridges]] [[ja:水神大橋]]

{{Cite sources|date=July 2024}} {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=461|align=left}} [[File:Suijin ohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View from Sumida Ward, Tokyo (January 2008)]] [[File:Midukamioohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View of Suijin Ōhashi from the Shuto Expressway (August 2005)]] [[File:100 views edo 037.jpg|thumb|200px|Suijin no Watashi]]
'''Suijin Ōhashi''' (水神大橋) is a bridge spanning the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川}}, carrying a spur of the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Route 461|ja|東京都道461号吾妻橋伊興町線}}. The western end of the bridge is located in {{ill|Minami-Senju|ja|南千住}} 8-chōme, Arakawa Ward, while the eastern end is in Tsuijidō 2-chōme, Sumida Ward. The bridge's name originates from the nearby {{ill|Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|ja|隅田川神社}}, also known as Suijin-gū.
The bridge was first constructed in March 1989 (Heisei 1). Its assembly was carried out on land, and the bridge girders were transported on barges to the site, where tidal changes were used to place them on the piers.
Nearby parks, {{ill|Shioiri Park|ja|汐入公園}} and {{ill|Higashi Shirahige Park|ja|東白鬚公園}}, serve as disaster response bases for Tokyo. As part of the disaster preparedness plan, Suijin Ōhashi was built alongside {{ill|Senju Shioiri Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋}} to connect these parks. Initially a pedestrian-only bridge, it was opened to vehicular traffic on July 10, 1996 (Heisei 8), following the completion of the connecting roads.
On its eastern side, Suijin Ōhashi connects to the {{ill|Shuto Expressway Mukōjima Line|ja|首都高速6号向島線}} at the {{ill|Tsuijidō Entrance|ja|堤通出入口}}.
This location was historically home to the ferry crossing "{{ill|Suijin no Watashi|ja|水神の渡し}}."
== Bridge Specifications == * **Type**: Steel road bridge * **Structure**: Three-span continuous Nielsen-Lohse girder arch bridge * **Length**: 157.0 meters * **Main span**: 102.0 meters * **Width**: 17.0 meters * **Construction started**: 1984 (Shōwa 59) * **Completed**: March 1989 (Heisei 1) * **Project owner**: Tokyo Metropolitan Government * **Design**: Tokyo Engineering * **Girder fabrication**: {{ill|Mitsubishi Heavy Industries|ja|三菱重工業}}
== Public Transportation == * **{{ill|Toei Bus|ja|都営バス}}** - {{ill|Route 40|ja|都営バス南千住営業所#錦40系統}}: {{ill|Minami-Senju Station|ja|南千住駅}} East Exit - Sumida 2-chōme - Shirahige Bridge - Higashi Mukōjima Hirokōji - {{ill|Tokyo Skytree Station|ja|とうきょうスカイツリー駅}} Front - {{ill|Kinshichō Station|ja|錦糸町駅}} Front
== Nearby Bridges == * {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川}} : (Upstream) - {{ill|Sumida River Bridge (Tokyo Metro Hibiya Line)|ja|隅田川橋梁 (東京地下鉄日比谷線)}} - {{ill|Senju Shioiri Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋}} - '''Suijin Ōhashi''' - {{ill|Shirahige Bridge|ja|白鬚橋}} - {{ill|Sakura Bridge (Tokyo)|ja|桜橋 (東京都)}} - (Downstream)

{{Cite sources|date=July 2024}} {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=461|align=left}} [[File:Suijin ohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View from Sumida Ward, Tokyo (January 2008)]] [[File:Midukamioohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View of Suijin Ōhashi from the Shuto Expressway (August 2005)]] [[File:100 views edo 037.jpg|thumb|200px|Suijin no Watashi]]
'''Suijin Ōhashi''' (水神大橋) is a bridge spanning the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川}}, carrying a spur of the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Route 461|ja|東京都道461号吾妻橋伊興町線}}. The western end of the bridge is located in {{ill|Minami-Senju|ja|南千住}} 8-chōme, Arakawa Ward, while the eastern end is in Tsuijidō 2-chōme, Sumida Ward. The bridge's name originates from the nearby {{ill|Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|ja|隅田川神社}}, also known as Suijin-gū.
The bridge was first constructed in March 1989 (Heisei 1). Its assembly was carried out on land, and the bridge girders were transported on barges to the site, where tidal changes were used to place them on the piers.
Nearby parks, {{ill|Shioiri Park|ja|汐入公園}} and {{ill|Higashi Shirahige Park|ja|東白鬚公園}}, serve as disaster response bases for Tokyo. As part of the disaster preparedness plan, Suijin Ōhashi was built alongside {{ill|Senju Shioiri Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋}} to connect these parks. Initially a pedestrian-only bridge, it was opened to vehicular traffic on July 10, 1996 (Heisei 8), following the completion of the connecting roads.
On its eastern side, Suijin Ōhashi connects to the {{ill|Shuto Expressway Mukōjima Line|ja|首都高速6号向島線}} at the {{ill|Tsuijidō Entrance|ja|堤通出入口}}.
This location was historically home to the ferry crossing "{{ill|Suijin no Watashi|ja|水神の渡し}}."
== Bridge Specifications == * '''Type''': Steel road bridge * '''Structure''': Three-span continuous Nielsen-Lohse girder arch bridge * '''Length''': 157.0 meters * '''Main span''': 102.0 meters * '''Width''': 17.0 meters * '''Construction started''': 1984 (Shōwa 59) * '''Completed''': March 1989 (Heisei 1) * '''Project owner''': Tokyo Metropolitan Government * '''Design''': Tokyo Engineering * '''Girder fabrication''': {{ill|Mitsubishi Heavy Industries|ja|三菱重工業}}

The bridge's name originates from the nearby {{ill|Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|ja|隅田川神社}}, also known as Suijin-gū.
The bridge was first constructed in March 1989 (Heisei 1). Its assembly was carried out on land, and the bridge girders were transported on barges to the site, where tidal changes were used to place them on the piers.
Nearby parks, {{ill|Shioiri Park|ja|汐入公園}} and {{ill|Higashi Shirahige Park|ja|東白鬚公園}}, serve as disaster response bases for Tokyo. As part of the disaster preparedness plan, Suijin Ōhashi was built alongside {{ill|Senju Shioiri Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋}} to connect these parks. Initially a pedestrian-only bridge, it was opened to vehicular traffic on July 10, 1996 (Heisei 8), following the completion of the connecting roads.
On its eastern side, Suijin Ōhashi connects to the {{ill|Shuto Expressway Mukōjima Line|ja|首都高速6号向島線}} at the {{ill|Tsuijidō Entrance|ja|堤通出入口}}.
This location was historically home to the ferry crossing "{{ill|Suijin no Watashi|ja|水神の渡し}}."
== Bridge Specifications == * '''Type''': Steel road bridge * '''Structure''': Three-span continuous Nielsen-Lohse girder arch bridge * '''Length''': 157.0 meters * '''Main span''': 102.0 meters * '''Width''': 17.0 meters * '''Construction started''': 1984 (Shōwa 59) * '''Completed''': March 1989 (Heisei 1) * '''Project owner''': Tokyo Metropolitan Government * '''Design''': Tokyo Engineering * '''Girder fabrication''': {{ill|Mitsubishi Heavy Industries|ja|三菱重工業}}
== Public Transportation == * '''{{ill|Toei Bus|ja|都営バス}}''' - {{ill|Route 40|ja|都営バス南千住営業所#錦40系統}}: {{ill|Minami-Senju Station|ja|南千住駅}} East Exit - Sumida 2-chōme - Shirahige Bridge - Higashi Mukōjima Hirokōji - {{ill|Tokyo Skytree Station|ja|とうきょうスカイツリー駅}} Front - {{ill|Kinshichō Station|ja|錦糸町駅}} Front
== Nearby Bridges == * {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川}} : (Upstream) - {{ill|Sumida River Bridge (Tokyo Metro Hibiya Line)|ja|隅田川橋梁 (東京地下鉄日比谷線)}} - {{ill|Senju Shioiri Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋}} - '''Suijin Ōhashi''' - {{ill|Shirahige Bridge|ja|白鬚橋}} - {{ill|Sakura Bridge (Tokyo)|ja|桜橋 (東京都)}} - (Downstream)

{{Cite sources|date=July 2024}} {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=461|align=left}} [[File:Suijin ohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View from Sumida Ward, Tokyo (January 2008)]] [[File:Midukamioohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View of Suijin Ōhashi from the Shuto Expressway (August 2005)]] [[File:100 views edo 037.jpg|thumb|200px|Suijin no Watashi]]
'''Suijin Ōhashi''' (水神大橋) is a bridge spanning the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川}}, carrying a spur of the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Route 461|ja|東京都道461号吾妻橋伊興町線}}. The western end of the bridge is located in {{ill|Minami-Senju|ja|南千住}} 8-chōme, Arakawa Ward, while the eastern end is in Tsuijidō 2-chōme, Sumida Ward. The bridge's name originates from the nearby {{ill|Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|ja|隅田川神社}}, also known as Suijin-gū.
The bridge was first constructed in March 1989 (Heisei 1). Its assembly was carried out on land, and the bridge girders were transported on barges to the site, where tidal changes were used to place them on the piers.
Nearby parks, {{ill|Shioiri Park|ja|汐入公園}} and {{ill|Higashi Shirahige Park|ja|東白鬚公園}}, serve as disaster response bases for Tokyo. As part of the disaster preparedness plan, Suijin Ōhashi was built alongside {{ill|Senju Shioiri Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋}} to connect these parks. Initially a pedestrian-only bridge, it was opened to vehicular traffic on July 10, 1996 (Heisei 8), following the completion of the connecting roads.
On its eastern side, Suijin Ōhashi connects to the {{ill|Shuto Expressway Mukōjima Line|ja|首都高速6号向島線}} at the {{ill|Tsuijidō Entrance|ja|堤通出入口}}.
This location was historically home to the ferry crossing "{{ill|Suijin no Watashi|ja|水神の渡し}}."
== Bridge Specifications == * '''Type''': Steel road bridge * '''Structure''': Three-span continuous Nielsen-Lohse girder arch bridge * '''Length''': 157.0 meters * '''Main span''': 102.0 meters * '''Width''': 17.0 meters * '''Construction started''': 1984 (Shōwa 59) * '''Completed''': March 1989 (Heisei 1) * '''Project owner''': Tokyo Metropolitan Government * '''Design''': Tokyo Engineering * '''Girder fabrication''': {{ill|Mitsubishi Heavy Industries|ja|三菱重工業}}
== Public Transportation == * '''{{ill|Toei Bus|ja|都営バス}}''' - {{ill|Route 40|ja|都営バス南千住営業所#錦40系統}}: {{ill|Minami-Senju Station|ja|南千住駅}} East Exit - Sumida 2-chōme - Shirahige Bridge - Higashi Mukōjima Hirokōji - {{ill|Tokyo Skytree Station|ja|とうきょうスカイツリー駅}} Front - {{ill|Kinshichō Station|ja|錦糸町駅}} Front
== Nearby Bridges == * {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川}} : (Upstream) - {{ill|Sumida River Bridge (Tokyo Metro Hibiya Line)|ja|隅田川橋梁 (東京地下鉄日比谷線)}} - {{ill|Senju Shioiri Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋}} - '''Suijin Ōhashi''' - {{ill|Shirahige Bridge|ja|白鬚橋}} - {{ill|Sakura Bridge (Tokyo)|ja|桜橋 (東京都)}} - (Downstream)

{{Cite sources|date=July 2024}} {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=461|align=left}} [[File:Suijin ohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View from Sumida Ward, Tokyo (January 2008)]] [[File:Midukamioohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View of Suijin Ōhashi from the Shuto Expressway (August 2005)]] [[File:100 views edo 037.jpg|thumb|200px|Suijin no Watashi]]
'''Suijin Ōhashi''' (水神大橋) is a bridge spanning the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川}}, carrying a spur of the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Route 461|ja|東京都道461号吾妻橋伊興町線}}. The western end of the bridge is located in {{ill|Minami-Senju|ja|南千住}} 8-chōme, Arakawa Ward, while the eastern end is in Tsuijidō 2-chōme, Sumida Ward. The bridge's name originates from the nearby {{ill|Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|ja|隅田川神社}}, also known as Suijin-gū.
The bridge was first constructed in March 1989 (Heisei 1). Its assembly was carried out on land, and the bridge girders were transported on barges to the site, where tidal changes were used to place them on the piers.
Nearby parks, {{ill|Shioiri Park|ja|汐入公園}} and {{ill|Higashi Shirahige Park|ja|東白鬚公園}}, serve as disaster response bases for Tokyo. As part of the disaster preparedness plan, Suijin Ōhashi was built alongside {{ill|Senju Shioiri Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋}} to connect these parks. Initially a pedestrian-only bridge, it was opened to vehicular traffic on July 10, 1996 (Heisei 8), following the completion of the connecting roads.
On its eastern side, Suijin Ōhashi connects to the {{ill|Shuto Expressway Mukōjima Line|ja|首都高速6号向島線}} at the {{ill|Tsuijidō Entrance|ja|堤通出入口}}.
This location was historically home to the ferry crossing "{{ill|Suijin no Watashi|ja|水神の渡し}}."
== Bridge Specifications == * '''Type''': Steel road bridge * '''Structure''': Three-span continuous Nielsen-Lohse girder arch bridge * '''Length''': 157.0 meters * '''Main span''': 102.0 meters * '''Width''': 17.0 meters * '''Construction started''': 1984 (Shōwa 59) * '''Completed''': March 1989 (Heisei 1) * '''Project owner''': Tokyo Metropolitan Government * '''Design''': Tokyo Engineering * '''Girder fabrication''': {{ill|Mitsubishi Heavy Industries|ja|三菱重工業}}
== Public Transportation == * '''{{ill|Toei Bus|ja|都営バス}}''' - {{ill|Route 40|ja|都営バス南千住営業所#錦40系統}}: {{ill|Minami-Senju Station|ja|南千住駅}} East Exit - Sumida 2-chōme - Shirahige Bridge - Higashi Mukōjima Hirokōji - {{ill|Tokyo Skytree Station|ja|とうきょうスカイツリー駅}} Front - {{ill|Kinshichō Station|ja|錦糸町駅}} Front
== Nearby Bridges == * {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川}} : (Upstream) - {{ill|Sumida River Bridge (Tokyo Metro Hibiya Line)|ja|隅田川橋梁 (東京地下鉄日比谷線)}} - {{ill|Senju Shioiri Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋}} - '''Suijin Ōhashi''' - {{ill|Shirahige Bridge|ja|白鬚橋}} - {{ill|Sakura Bridge (Tokyo)|ja|桜橋 (東京都)}} - (Downstream)

{{Cite sources|date=July 2024}} {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=461|align=left}} [[File:Suijin ohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View from Sumida Ward, Tokyo (January 2008)]] [[File:Midukamioohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View of Suijin Ōhashi from the Shuto Expressway (August 2005)]] [[File:100 views edo 037.jpg|thumb|200px|Suijin no Watashi]]
'''Suijin Ōhashi''' (水神大橋) is a bridge spanning the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川}}, carrying a spur of the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Route 461|ja|東京都道461号吾妻橋伊興町線}}. The western end of the bridge is located in {{ill|Minami-Senju|ja|南千住}} 8-chōme, Arakawa Ward, while the eastern end is in Tsuijidō 2-chōme, Sumida Ward. The bridge's name originates from the nearby {{ill|Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|ja|隅田川神社}}, also known as Suijin-gū.
The bridge was first constructed in March 1989 (Heisei 1). Its assembly was carried out on land, and the bridge girders were transported on barges to the site, where tidal changes were used to place them on the piers.
Nearby parks, {{ill|Shioiri Park|ja|汐入公園}} and {{ill|Higashi Shirahige Park|ja|東白鬚公園}}, serve as disaster response bases for Tokyo. As part of the disaster preparedness plan, Suijin Ōhashi was built alongside {{ill|Senju Shioiri Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋}} to connect these parks. Initially a pedestrian-only bridge, it was opened to vehicular traffic on July 10, 1996 (Heisei 8), following the completion of the connecting roads.
On its eastern side, Suijin Ōhashi connects to the {{ill|Shuto Expressway Mukōjima Line|ja|首都高速6号向島線}} at the {{ill|Tsuijidō Entrance|ja|堤通出入口}}.
This location was historically home to the ferry crossing "{{ill|Suijin no Watashi|ja|水神の渡し}}."

Initially a pedestrian-only bridge, it was opened to vehicular traffic on July 10, 1996 (Heisei 8), following the completion of the connecting roads.

{{Cite sources|date=July 2024}} {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=461|align=left}} [[File:Suijin ohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View from Sumida Ward, Tokyo (January 2008)]] [[File:Midukamioohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View of Suijin Ōhashi from the Shuto Expressway (August 2005)]] [[File:100 views edo 037.jpg|thumb|200px|Suijin no Watashi]]
'''Suijin Ōhashi''' (水神大橋) is a bridge spanning the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川}}, carrying a spur of the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Route 461|ja|東京都道461号吾妻橋伊興町線}}. The western end of the bridge is located in {{ill|Minami-Senju|ja|南千住}} 8-chōme, Arakawa Ward, while the eastern end is in Tsuijidō 2-chōme, Sumida Ward. The bridge's name originates from the nearby {{ill|Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|ja|隅田川神社}}, also known as Suijin-gū.
The bridge was first constructed in March 1989 (Heisei 1). Its assembly was carried out on land, and the bridge girders were transported on barges to the site, where tidal changes were used to place them on the piers.
Nearby parks, {{ill|Shioiri Park|ja|汐入公園}} and {{ill|Higashi Shirahige Park|ja|東白鬚公園}}, serve as disaster response bases for Tokyo. As part of the disaster preparedness plan, Suijin Ōhashi was built alongside {{ill|Senju Shioiri Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋}} to connect these parks. Initially a pedestrian-only bridge, it was opened to vehicular traffic on July 10, 1996 (Heisei 8), following the completion of the connecting roads.
On its eastern side, Suijin Ōhashi connects to the {{ill|Shuto Expressway Mukōjima Line|ja|首都高速6号向島線}} at the {{ill|Tsuijidō Entrance|ja|堤通出入口}}.
This location was historically home to the ferry crossing "{{ill|Suijin no Watashi|ja|水神の渡し}}."
== Bridge Specifications == * '''Type''': Steel road bridge * '''Structure''': Three-span continuous Nielsen-Lohse girder arch bridge * '''Length''': 157.0 meters * '''Main span''': 102.0 meters * '''Width''': 17.0 meters * '''Construction started''': 1984 (Shōwa 59) * '''Completed''': March 1989 (Heisei 1) * '''Project owner''': Tokyo Metropolitan Government * '''Design''': Tokyo Engineering * '''Girder fabrication''': {{ill|Mitsubishi Heavy Industries|ja|三菱重工業}}
== Public Transportation == * '''{{ill|Toei Bus|ja|都営バス}}''' - {{ill|Route 40|ja|都営バス南千住営業所#錦40系統}}: {{ill|Minami-Senju Station|ja|南千住駅}} East Exit - Sumida 2-chōme - Shirahige Bridge - Higashi Mukōjima Hirokōji - {{ill|Tokyo Skytree Station|ja|とうきょうスカイツリー駅}} Front - {{ill|Kinshichō Station|ja|錦糸町駅}} Front
== Nearby Bridges == * {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川}} : (Upstream) - {{ill|Sumida River Bridge (Tokyo Metro Hibiya Line)|ja|隅田川橋梁 (東京地下鉄日比谷線)}} - {{ill|Senju Shioiri Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋}} - '''Suijin Ōhashi''' - {{ill|Shirahige Bridge|ja|白鬚橋}} - {{ill|Sakura Bridge (Tokyo)|ja|桜橋 (東京都)}} - (Downstream)

{{Cite sources|date=July 2024}} {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=461|align=left}} [[File:Suijin ohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View from Sumida Ward, Tokyo (January 2008)]] [[File:Midukamioohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View of Suijin Ōhashi from the Shuto Expressway (August 2005)]] [[File:100 views edo 037.jpg|thumb|200px|Suijin no Watashi]]
'''Suijin Ōhashi''' (水神大橋) is a bridge spanning the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川}}, carrying a spur of the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Route 461|ja|東京都道461号吾妻橋伊興町線}}. The western end of the bridge is located in {{ill|Minami-Senju|ja|南千住}} 8-chōme, Arakawa Ward, while the eastern end is in Tsuijidō 2-chōme, Sumida Ward. The bridge's name originates from the nearby {{ill|Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|ja|隅田川神社}}, also known as Suijin-gū.
The bridge was first constructed in March 1989 (Heisei 1). Its assembly was carried out on land, and the bridge girders were transported on barges to the site, where tidal changes were used to place them on the piers.
Nearby parks, {{ill|Shioiri Park|ja|汐入公園}} and {{ill|Higashi Shirahige Park|ja|東白鬚公園}}, serve as disaster response bases for Tokyo. As part of the disaster preparedness plan, Suijin Ōhashi was built alongside {{ill|Senju Shioiri Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋}} to connect these parks. Initially a pedestrian-only bridge, it was opened to vehicular traffic on July 10, 1996 (Heisei 8), following the completion of the connecting roads.
On its eastern side, Suijin Ōhashi connects to the {{ill|Shuto Expressway Mukōjima Line|ja|首都高速6号向島線}} at the {{ill|Tsuijidō Entrance|ja|堤通出入口}}.
This location was historically home to the ferry crossing "{{ill|Suijin no Watashi|ja|水神の渡し}}."
== Bridge Specifications == * '''Type''': Steel road bridge * '''Structure''': Three-span continuous Nielsen-Lohse girder arch bridge * '''Length''': 157.0 meters * '''Main span''': 102.0 meters * '''Width''': 17.0 meters * '''Construction started''': 1984 (Shōwa 59) * '''Completed''': March 1989 (Heisei 1) * '''Project owner''': Tokyo Metropolitan Government * '''Design''': Tokyo Engineering * '''Girder fabrication''': {{ill|Mitsubishi Heavy Industries|ja|三菱重工業}}
== Public Transportation == * '''{{ill|Toei Bus|ja|都営バス}}''' - {{ill|Route 40|ja|都営バス南千住営業所#錦40系統}}: {{ill|Minami-Senju Station|ja|南千住駅}} East Exit - Sumida 2-chōme - Shirahige Bridge - Higashi Mukōjima Hirokōji - {{ill|Tokyo Skytree Station|ja|とうきょうスカイツリー駅}} Front - {{ill|Kinshichō Station|ja|錦糸町駅}} Front
== Nearby Bridges == * {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川}} : (Upstream) - {{ill|Sumida River Bridge (Tokyo Metro Hibiya Line)|ja|隅田川橋梁 (東京地下鉄日比谷線)}} - {{ill|Senju Shioiri Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋}} - '''Suijin Ōhashi''' - {{ill|Shirahige Bridge|ja|白鬚橋}} - {{ill|Sakura Bridge (Tokyo)|ja|桜橋 (東京都)}} - (Downstream)
{{Sumida River Bridges}} {{commonscat|Suijin Great Bridge}} {{Coord|35|44|10|N|139|48|51|E|display=title|region:JP_type:landmark}}

{{Cite sources|date=July 2024}} {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=461|align=left}} [[File:Suijin ohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View from Sumida Ward, Tokyo (January 2008)]] [[File:Midukamioohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View of Suijin Ōhashi from the Shuto Expressway (August 2005)]] [[File:100 views edo 037.jpg|thumb|200px|Suijin no Watashi]]
'''Suijin Ōhashi''' (水神大橋) is a bridge spanning the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川}}, carrying a spur of the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Route 461|ja|東京都道461号吾妻橋伊興町線}}. The western end of the bridge is located in {{ill|Minami-Senju|ja|南千住}} 8-chōme, Arakawa Ward, while the eastern end is in Tsuijidō 2-chōme, Sumida Ward. The bridge's name originates from the nearby {{ill|Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|ja|隅田川神社}}, also known as Suijin-gū.
The bridge was first constructed in March 1989 (Heisei 1). Its assembly was carried out on land, and the bridge girders were transported on barges to the site, where tidal changes were used to place them on the piers.
Nearby parks, {{ill|Shioiri Park|ja|汐入公園}} and {{ill|Higashi Shirahige Park|ja|東白鬚公園}}, serve as disaster response bases for Tokyo. As part of the disaster preparedness plan, Suijin Ōhashi was built alongside {{ill|Senju Shioiri Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋}} to connect these parks. Initially a pedestrian-only bridge, it was opened to vehicular traffic on July 10, 1996 (Heisei 8), following the completion of the connecting roads.
On its eastern side, Suijin Ōhashi connects to the {{ill|Shuto Expressway Mukōjima Line|ja|首都高速6号向島線}} at the {{ill|Tsuijidō Entrance|ja|堤通出入口}}.
This location was historically home to the ferry crossing "{{ill|Suijin no Watashi|ja|水神の渡し}}."
== Bridge Specifications == * '''Type''': Steel road bridge * '''Structure''': Three-span continuous Nielsen-Lohse girder arch bridge * '''Length''': 157.0 meters * '''Main span''': 102.0 meters * '''Width''': 17.0 meters * '''Construction started''': 1984 (Shōwa 59) * '''Completed''': March 1989 (Heisei 1) * '''Project owner''': Tokyo Metropolitan Government * '''Design''': Tokyo Engineering * '''Girder fabrication''': {{ill|Mitsubishi Heavy Industries|ja|三菱重工業}}
== Public Transportation == * '''{{ill|Toei Bus|ja|都営バス}}''' - {{ill|Route 40|ja|都営バス南千住営業所#錦40系統}}: {{ill|Minami-Senju Station|ja|南千住駅}} East Exit - Sumida 2-chōme - Shirahige Bridge - Higashi Mukōjima Hirokōji - {{ill|Tokyo Skytree Station|ja|とうきょうスカイツリー駅}} Front - {{ill|Kinshichō Station|ja|錦糸町駅}} Front
== Nearby Bridges == * {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川}} : (Upstream) - {{ill|Sumida River Bridge (Tokyo Metro Hibiya Line)|ja|隅田川橋梁 (東京地下鉄日比谷線)}} - {{ill|Senju Shioiri Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋}} - '''Suijin Ōhashi''' - {{ill|Shirahige Bridge|ja|白鬚橋}} - {{ill|Sakura Bridge (Tokyo)|ja|桜橋 (東京都)}} - (Downstream)

{{Cite sources|date=July 2024}} {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=461|align=left}} [[File:Suijin ohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View from Sumida Ward, Tokyo (January 2008)]] [[File:Midukamioohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View of Suijin Ōhashi from the Shuto Expressway (August 2005)]] [[File:100 views edo 037.jpg|thumb|200px|Suijin no Watashi]]
'''Suijin Ōhashi''' (水神大橋) is a bridge spanning the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川}}, carrying a spur of the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Route 461|ja|東京都道461号吾妻橋伊興町線}}. The western end of the bridge is located in {{ill|Minami-Senju|ja|南千住}} 8-chōme, Arakawa Ward, while the eastern end is in Tsuijidō 2-chōme, Sumida Ward. The bridge's name originates from the nearby {{ill|Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|ja|隅田川神社}}, also known as Suijin-gū.
The bridge was first constructed in March 1989 (Heisei 1). Its assembly was carried out on land, and the bridge girders were transported on barges to the site, where tidal changes were used to place them on the piers.
Nearby parks, {{ill|Shioiri Park|ja|汐入公園}} and {{ill|Higashi Shirahige Park|ja|東白鬚公園}}, serve as disaster response bases for Tokyo. As part of the disaster preparedness plan, Suijin Ōhashi was built alongside {{ill|Senju Shioiri Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋}} to connect these parks. Initially a pedestrian-only bridge, it was opened to vehicular traffic on July 10, 1996 (Heisei 8), following the completion of the connecting roads.
On its eastern side, Suijin Ōhashi connects to the {{ill|Shuto Expressway Mukōjima Line|ja|首都高速6号向島線}} at the {{ill|Tsuijidō Entrance|ja|堤通出入口}}.
This location was historically home to the ferry crossing "{{ill|Suijin no Watashi|ja|水神の渡し}}."
== Bridge Specifications == * '''Type''': Steel road bridge * '''Structure''': Three-span continuous Nielsen-Lohse girder arch bridge * '''Length''': 157.0 meters * '''Main span''': 102.0 meters * '''Width''': 17.0 meters * '''Construction started''': 1984 (Shōwa 59) * '''Completed''': March 1989 (Heisei 1) * '''Project owner''': Tokyo Metropolitan Government * '''Design''': Tokyo Engineering * '''Girder fabrication''': {{ill|Mitsubishi Heavy Industries|ja|三菱重工業}}

{{Cite sources|date=July 2024}} {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=461|align=left}} [[File:Suijin ohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View from Sumida Ward, Tokyo (January 2008)]] [[File:Midukamioohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View of Suijin Ōhashi from the Shuto Expressway (August 2005)]] [[File:100 views edo 037.jpg|thumb|200px|Suijin no Watashi]]
'''Suijin Ōhashi''' (水神大橋) is a bridge spanning the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川}}, carrying a spur of the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Route 461|ja|東京都道461号吾妻橋伊興町線}}. The western end of the bridge is located in {{ill|Minami-Senju|ja|南千住}} 8-chōme, Arakawa Ward, while the eastern end is in Tsuijidō 2-chōme, Sumida Ward. The bridge's name originates from the nearby {{ill|Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|ja|隅田川神社}}, also known as Suijin-gū.
The bridge was first constructed in March 1989 (Heisei 1). Its assembly was carried out on land, and the bridge girders were transported on barges to the site, where tidal changes were used to place them on the piers.
Nearby parks, {{ill|Shioiri Park|ja|汐入公園}} and {{ill|Higashi Shirahige Park|ja|東白鬚公園}}, serve as disaster response bases for Tokyo. As part of the disaster preparedness plan, Suijin Ōhashi was built alongside {{ill|Senju Shioiri Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋}} to connect these parks. Initially a pedestrian-only bridge, it was opened to vehicular traffic on July 10, 1996 (Heisei 8), following the completion of the connecting roads.
On its eastern side, Suijin Ōhashi connects to the {{ill|Shuto Expressway Mukōjima Line|ja|首都高速6号向島線}} at the {{ill|Tsuijidō Entrance|ja|堤通出入口}}.
This location was historically home to the ferry crossing "{{ill|Suijin no Watashi|ja|水神の渡し}}."
== Bridge Specifications == * '''Type''': Steel road bridge * '''Structure''': Three-span continuous Nielsen-Lohse girder arch bridge * '''Length''': 157.0 meters * '''Main span''': 102.0 meters * '''Width''': 17.0 meters * '''Construction started''': 1984 (Shōwa 59) * '''Completed''': March 1989 (Heisei 1) * '''Project owner''': Tokyo Metropolitan Government * '''Design''': Tokyo Engineering * '''Girder fabrication''': {{ill|Mitsubishi Heavy Industries|ja|三菱重工業}}
== Public Transportation == * '''{{ill|Toei Bus|ja|都営バス}}''' - {{ill|Route 40|ja|都営バス南千住営業所#錦40系統}}: {{ill|Minami-Senju Station|ja|南千住駅}} East Exit - Sumida 2-chōme - Shirahige Bridge - Higashi Mukōjima Hirokōji - {{ill|Tokyo Skytree Station|ja|とうきょうスカイツリー駅}} Front - {{ill|Kinshichō Station|ja|錦糸町駅}} Front
== Nearby Bridges == * {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川}} : (Upstream) - {{ill|Sumida River Bridge (Tokyo Metro Hibiya Line)|ja|隅田川橋梁 (東京地下鉄日比谷線)}} - {{ill|Senju Shioiri Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋}} * '''Suijin Ōhashi''' - {{ill|Shirahige Bridge|ja|白鬚橋}} - {{ill|Sakura Bridge (Tokyo)|ja|桜橋 (東京都)}} - (Downstream)

{{Cite sources|date=July 2024}} {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=461|align=left}} [[File:Suijin ohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View from Sumida Ward, Tokyo (January 2008)]] [[File:Midukamioohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View of Suijin Ōhashi from the Shuto Expressway (August 2005)]] [[File:100 views edo 037.jpg|thumb|200px|Suijin no Watashi]]
'''Suijin Ōhashi''' (水神大橋) is a bridge spanning the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川}}, carrying a spur of the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Route 461|ja|東京都道461号吾妻橋伊興町線}}. The western end of the bridge is located in {{ill|Minami-Senju|ja|南千住}} 8-chōme, Arakawa Ward, while the eastern end is in Tsuijidō 2-chōme, Sumida Ward. The bridge's name originates from the nearby {{ill|Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|ja|隅田川神社}}, also known as Suijin-gū.
The bridge was first constructed in March 1989 (Heisei 1). Its assembly was carried out on land, and the bridge girders were transported on barges to the site, where tidal changes were used to place them on the piers.
Nearby parks, {{ill|Shioiri Park|ja|汐入公園}} and {{ill|Higashi Shirahige Park|ja|東白鬚公園}}, serve as disaster response bases for Tokyo. As part of the disaster preparedness plan, Suijin Ōhashi was built alongside {{ill|Senju Shioiri Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋}} to connect these parks. Initially a pedestrian-only bridge, it was opened to vehicular traffic on July 10, 1996 (Heisei 8), following the completion of the connecting roads.
On its eastern side, Suijin Ōhashi connects to the {{ill|Shuto Expressway Mukōjima Line|ja|首都高速6号向島線}} at the {{ill|Tsuijidō Entrance|ja|堤通出入口}}.
This location was historically home to the ferry crossing "{{ill|Suijin no Watashi|ja|水神の渡し}}."
== Bridge Specifications == * '''Type''': Steel road bridge * '''Structure''': Three-span continuous Nielsen-Lohse girder arch bridge * '''Length''': 157.0 meters * '''Main span''': 102.0 meters * '''Width''': 17.0 meters * '''Construction started''': 1984 (Shōwa 59) * '''Completed''': March 1989 (Heisei 1) * '''Project owner''': Tokyo Metropolitan Government * '''Design''': Tokyo Engineering * '''Girder fabrication''': {{ill|Mitsubishi Heavy Industries|ja|三菱重工業}}
== Public Transportation == * '''{{ill|Toei Bus|ja|都営バス}}''' - {{ill|Route 40|ja|都営バス南千住営業所#錦40系統}}: {{ill|Minami-Senju Station|ja|南千住駅}} East Exit - Sumida 2-chōme - Shirahige Bridge - Higashi Mukōjima Hirokōji - {{ill|Tokyo Skytree Station|ja|とうきょうスカイツリー駅}} Front - {{ill|Kinshichō Station|ja|錦糸町駅}} Front
== Nearby Bridges == * {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川}} : (Upstream) - {{ill|Sumida River Bridge (Tokyo Metro Hibiya Line)|ja|隅田川橋梁 (東京地下鉄日比谷線)}} - {{ill|Senju Shioiri Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋}} * '''Suijin Ōhashi''' - {{ill|Shirahige Bridge|ja|白鬚橋}} - {{ill|Sakura Bridge (Tokyo)|ja|桜橋 (東京都)}} - (Downstream)

'''Suijin Ōhashi''' (水神大橋) is a bridge spanning the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川}}, carrying a spur of the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Route 461|ja|東京都道461号吾妻橋伊興町線}}. The western end of the bridge is located in {{ill|Minami-Senju|ja|南千住}} 8-chōme, Arakawa Ward, while the eastern end is in Tsuijidō 2-chōme, Sumida Ward. The bridge's name originates from the nearby {{ill|Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|ja|隅田川神社}}, also known as Suijin-gū.
The bridge was first constructed in March 1989 (Heisei 1). Its assembly was carried out on land, and the bridge girders were transported on barges to the site, where tidal changes were used to place them on the piers.
Nearby parks, {{ill|Shioiri Park|ja|汐入公園}} and {{ill|Higashi Shirahige Park|ja|東白鬚公園}}, serve as disaster response bases for Tokyo. The bridge was first constructed in March 1989 (Heisei 1). Its assembly was carried out on land, and the bridge girders were transported on barges to the site, where tidal changes were used to place them on the piers.
Nearby parks, {{ill|Shioiri Park|ja|汐入公園}} and {{ill|Higashi Shirahige Park|ja|東白鬚公園}}, serve as disaster response bases for Tokyo. As part of the disaster preparedness plan, Suijin Ōhashi was built alongside {{ill|Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋}} to connect these parks. Initially a pedestrian-only bridge, it was opened to vehicular traffic on July 10, 1996 (Heisei 8), following the completion tier 1]][[Category: of the connecting roads.
On its eastern side, Suijin Ōhashi connects to the {{ill|Shuto Expressway Mukōjima Line|ja|首都高速6号向島線}} at the {{ill|Tsuijidō Entrance|ja|堤通出入口}}.

{{Cite sources|date=July 2024}} {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=461|align=left}} [[File:Suijin ohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View from Sumida Ward, Tokyo (January 2008)]] [[File:Midukamioohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View of Suijin Ōhashi from the Shuto Expressway (August 2005)]] [[File:100 views edo 037.jpg|thumb|200px|Suijin no Watashi]]
'''Suijin Ōhashi''' (水神大橋) is a bridge spanning the {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川}}, carrying a spur of the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Route 461|ja|東京都道461号吾妻橋伊興町線}}. The western end of the bridge is located in {{ill|Minami-Senju|ja|南千住}} 8-chōme, Arakawa Ward, while the eastern end is in Tsuijidō 2-chōme, Sumida Ward. The bridge's name originates from the nearby {{ill|Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|ja|隅田川神社}}, also known as Suijin-gū.
The bridge was first constructed in March 1989 (Heisei 1). Its assembly was carried out on land, and the bridge girders were transported on barges to the site, where tidal changes were used to place them on the piers.
Nearby parks, {{ill|Shioiri Park|ja|汐入公園}} and {{ill|Higashi Shirahige Park|ja|東白鬚公園}}, serve as disaster response bases for Tokyo. As part of the disaster preparedness plan, Suijin Ōhashi was built alongside {{ill|Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋}} to connect these parks. Initially a pedestrian-only bridge, it was opened to vehicular traffic on July 10, 1996 (Heisei 8), following the completion tier 1]][[Category: of the connecting roads.
On its eastern side, Suijin Ōhashi connects to the {{ill|Shuto Expressway Mukōjima Line|ja|首都高速6号向島線}} at the {{ill|Tsuijidō Entrance|ja|堤通出入口}}.
This location was historically home to the ferry crossing "{{ill|Suijin no Watashi|ja|水神の渡し}}."
== Bridge Specifications == * '''Type''': Steel road bridge * '''Structure''': Three-span continuous Nielsen-Lohse girder arch bridge * '''Length''': 157.0 meters * '''Main span''': 102.0 meters * '''Width''': 17.0 meters * '''Construction started''': 1984 (Shōwa 59) * '''Completed''': March 1989 (Heisei 1) * '''Project owner''': Tokyo Metropolitan Government * '''Design''': Tokyo Engineering * '''Girder fabrication''': {{ill|Mitsubishi Heavy Industries|ja|三菱重工業}}
== Public Transportation == * '''{{ill|Toei Bus|ja|都営バス}}''' ** {{ill|Route 40|ja|都営バス南千住営業所#錦40系統}}: {{ill|Minami-Senju Station|ja|南千住駅}} East Exit - Sumida 2-chōme - Shirahige Bridge - Higashi Mukōjima Hirokōji - {{ill|Tokyo Skytree Station|ja|とうきょうスカイツリー駅}} Front - {{ill|Kinshichō Station|ja|錦糸町駅}} Front
== Nearby Bridges == * {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川}} : (Upstream) - {{ill|Sumida River Bridge (Tokyo Metro Hibiya Line)|ja|隅田川橋梁 (東京地下鉄日比谷線)}} - {{ill|Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋}} * '''Suijin Ōhashi''' - {{ill|Shirahige Bridge|ja|白鬚橋}} - {{ill|Sakura Bridge (Tokyo)|ja|桜橋 (東京都)}} - (Downstream)

== Nearby Bridges == * {{ill|Sumida River|ja|隅田川|12=draft}} : (Upstream) - {{ill|Sumida River Bridge (Tokyo Metro Hibiya Line)|ja|隅田川橋梁 (東京地下鉄日比谷線)|12=draft}} - {{ill|Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋|12=draft}} * '''Suijin Ōhashi''' - {{ill|Shirahige Bridge|ja|白鬚橋|12=draft}} - {{ill|Sakura Bridge (Tokyo)|ja|桜橋 (東京都)|12=draft}} - (Downstream)

37 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:水神大橋 up to rev 101205972
revision.text:
{{出典の明記| date = 2024年7月}} {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=東京|number=461|align=left}} [[ファイル:Suijin ohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|東京都墨田区から（2008年1月）]] [[ファイル:Midukamioohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|首都高速上より眺める水神大橋（2005年8月）]] [[ファイル:100 views edo 037.jpg|サムネイル|200px|水神の渡し]] '''水神大橋'''（すいじんおおはし）は、[[隅田川]]にかかる[[東京都道461号吾妻橋伊興町線]]支線の[[橋]]。西岸は[[荒川区]][[南千住]]八丁目、東岸は[[墨田区]]堤通二丁目。橋名は東岸にある「[[隅田川神社]]（水神宮）」に因む。
創架は[[1989年]]（[[平成]]元年）3月。橋の組み立てを陸上で行い、橋桁を台船に乗せて干満の差を利用して橋脚に乗せるという方法を利用した。
付近の[[汐入公園]]、[[東白鬚公園]]は東京都の防災拠点として位置づけられており、そのため双方を繋ぐ連絡橋として[[千住汐入大橋]]と共に計画された。当初は[[人道橋|歩行者専用橋]]で、接続する道路の整備が完了した[[1996年]]（平成8年）7月10日より自動車道路供用が開始された。
橋の東詰で、[[首都高速6号向島線]][[堤通出入口]]に接続する。
もともと「[[隅田川の渡し|水神の渡し]]」という渡船場があった場所でもある。
== 橋の概要 == *種別 - 鋼道路橋 *形式 - 3径間連続[[アーチ橋#補剛桁を有するアーチ|ニールセンローゼ桁橋]] *橋長 - 157.0m *最大支間 - 102.0m *幅員 - 17.0m *着工 - [[昭和]]59年（1984年） *竣工 - [[平成]]元年（1989年）3月 *事業主体 - 東京都 *橋梁設計 - 東京エンジニアリング *橋桁製作 - [[三菱重工業]]
== 路線バス == * [[都営バス]] ** [[都営バス南千住営業所#錦40系統|錦40]] [[南千住駅]]東口 - 墨田二丁目 - 白鬚橋 - 東向島広小路 - [[とうきょうスカイツリー駅]]前 - [[錦糸町駅]]前
== 隣の橋 == * [[隅田川]] : （上流） - [[隅田川橋梁 (東京地下鉄日比谷線)|隅田川橋梁]] - [[千住汐入大橋]] - '''水神大橋''' - [[白鬚橋]] - [[桜橋 (東京都)|桜橋]] - （下流）
{{隅田川の橋}} {{commonscat|Suijin Great Bridge}}
{{Coord|35|44|10|N|139|48|51|E|display=title|region:JP_type:landmark}} {{DEFAULTSORT:すいしんおおはし}} [[Category:東京都区部の橋]] [[Category:荒川区の交通]] [[Category:南千住]] [[Category:墨田区の橋]] [[Category:隅田川の橋]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Suijin Great Bridge]][[ja:水神大橋]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11549046]] Wikidata name: Suijin Ōhashi Bridge [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=July 2024}} {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=461|align=left}} [[File:Suijin ohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View from Sumida Ward, Tokyo (January 2008)]] [[File:Midukamioohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View of Suijin Ōhashi from the Shuto Expressway (August 2005)]] [[File:100 views edo 037.jpg|thumb|200px|Suijin no Watashi]]
'''Suijin Ōhashi''' (水神大橋) is a bridge spanning the [[:en:Sumida River|Sumida River]], carrying a spur of the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Route 461|ja|東京都道461号吾妻橋伊興町線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Metropolitan Route 461|qq=|1=Tokyo Metropolitan Road Route 461|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525822}}. The western end of the bridge is located in {{ill|Minami-Senju|ja|南千住|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minami-Senju|qq=|1=Minami-Senju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11407548}} 8-chōme, Arakawa Ward, while the eastern end is in Tsuijidō 2-chōme, Sumida Ward. The bridge's name originates from the nearby {{ill|Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|ja|隅田川神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|qq=|1=Sumidagawa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658854}}, also known as Suijin-gū.
The bridge was first constructed in March 1989 (Heisei 1). Its assembly was carried out on land, and the bridge girders were transported on barges to the site, where tidal changes were used to place them on the piers.
Nearby parks, {{ill|Shioiri Park|ja|汐入公園|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioiri Park|qq=|1=Shioiri Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11550497}} and {{ill|Higashi Shirahige Park|ja|東白鬚公園|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Shirahige Park|qq=|1=Higashi-Shirahige Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21019411}}, serve as disaster response bases for Tokyo. As part of the disaster preparedness plan, Suijin Ōhashi was built alongside {{ill|Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|qq=|1=Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11405350}} to connect these parks. Initially a pedestrian-only bridge, it was opened to vehicular traffic on July 10, 1996 (Heisei 8), following the completion of the connecting roads.
On its eastern side, Suijin Ōhashi connects to the [[:en:Mukojima Route|Shuto Expressway Mukōjima Line]] at the {{ill|Tsuijidō Entrance|ja|堤通出入口|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuijidō Entrance|qq=}}.
This location was historically home to the ferry crossing "{{ill|Suijin no Watashi|ja|水神の渡し|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suijin no Watashi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}."
== Bridge Specifications == * '''Type''': Steel road bridge * '''Structure''': Three-span continuous Nielsen-Lohse girder arch bridge * '''Length''': 157.0 meters * '''Main span''': 102.0 meters * '''Width''': 17.0 meters * '''Construction started''': 1984 (Shōwa 59) * '''Completed''': March 1989 (Heisei 1) * '''Project owner''': Tokyo Metropolitan Government * '''Design''': Tokyo Engineering * '''Girder fabrication''': [[:en:Mitsubishi Heavy Industries|Mitsubishi Heavy Industries]]
== Public Transportation == * '''[[:en:Toei Bus|Toei Bus]]''' ** {{ill|Route 40|ja|都営バス南千住営業所#錦40系統|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Route 40|qq=|1=Toei Bus Minami-Senju Office|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11643502}}: [[:en:Minami-Senju Station|Minami-Senju Station]] East Exit - Sumida 2-chōme - Shirahige Bridge - Higashi Mukōjima Hirokōji - [[:en:Tokyo Skytree Station|Tokyo Skytree Station]] Front - [[:en:Kinshichō Station|Kinshichō Station]] Front
== Nearby Bridges == * [[:en:Sumida River|Sumida River]] : (Upstream) - {{ill|Sumida River Bridge (Tokyo Metro Hibiya Line)|ja|隅田川橋梁 (東京地下鉄日比谷線)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumida River Bridge (Tokyo Metro Hibiya Line)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} - {{ill|Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|qq=|1=Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11405350}} * '''Suijin Ōhashi''' - {{ill|Shirahige Bridge|ja|白鬚橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirahige Bridge|qq=}} - {{ill|Sakura Bridge (Tokyo)|ja|桜橋 (東京都)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakura Bridge (Tokyo)|qq=|1=Sakura Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538116}} - (Downstream)
{{Sumida River Bridges}} {{commonscat|Suijin Great Bridge}} {{Coord|35|44|10|N|139|48|51|E|display=title|region:JP_type:landmark}}
}}

Nearby parks, {{ill|Shioiri Park|ja|汐入公園|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioiri Park|qq=|1=Shioiri Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11550497}} and {{ill|Higashi Shirahige Park|ja|東白鬚公園|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Shirahige Park|qq=|1=Higashi-Shirahige Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21019411}}, serve as disaster response bases for Tokyo. As part of the disaster preparedness plan, Suijin Ōhashi was built alongside {{ill|Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|qq=|1=Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11405350}} to connect these parks. Initially a pedestrian-only bridge, it was opened to vehicular traffic on July 10, 1996 (Heisei 8), following the completion of the connecting roads.
On its eastern side, Suijin Ōhashi connects to the [[:en:Mukojima Route|Shuto Expressway Mukōjima Line]] at the {{ill|Tsuijidō Entrance|ja|堤通出入口|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuijidō Entrance|qq=}}.

'''Suijin Ōhashi''' (水神大橋) is a bridge spanning the [[:en:Sumida River|Sumida River]], carrying a spur of the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Route 461|ja|東京都道461号吾妻橋伊興町線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Metropolitan Route 461|1=Tokyo Metropolitan Road Route 461|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525822}}. The bridge's name originates from the nearby {{ill|Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|ja|隅田川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|1=Sumidagawa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658854}}, also known as Suijin-gū.
The bridge was first constructed in March 1989 (Heisei 1). Its assembly was carried out on land, and the bridge girders were transported on barges to the site, where tidal changes were used to place them on the piers.
Nearby parks, {{ill|Shioiri Park|ja|汐入公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioiri Park|1=Shioiri Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11550497}} and {{ill|Higashi Shirahige Park|ja|東白鬚公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Shirahige Park|1=Higashi-Shirahige Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21019411}}, serve as disaster response bases for Tokyo. Initially a pedestrian-only bridge, it was opened to vehicular traffic on July 10, 1996 (Heisei 8), following the completion of the connecting roads.
On its eastern side, Suijin Ōhashi connects to the [[:en:Mukojima Route|Shuto Expressway Mukōjima Line]] at the {{ill|Tsuijidō Entrance|ja|堤通出入口|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuijidō Entrance|qq=}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Suijin Great Bridge]][[ja:水神大橋]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11549046]] Wikidata name: Suijin Ōhashi Bridge [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=July 2024}} {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=461|align=left}} [[File:Suijin ohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View from Sumida Ward, Tokyo (January 2008)]] [[File:Midukamioohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View of Suijin Ōhashi from the Shuto Expressway (August 2005)]] [[File:100 views edo 037.jpg|thumb|200px|Suijin no Watashi]]
'''Suijin Ōhashi''' (水神大橋) is a bridge spanning the [[:en:Sumida River|Sumida River]], carrying a spur of the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Route 461|ja|東京都道461号吾妻橋伊興町線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Metropolitan Route 461|1=Tokyo Metropolitan Road Route 461|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525822}}. The western end of the bridge is located in {{ill|Minami-Senju|ja|南千住|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minami-Senju|1=Minami-Senju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11407548}} 8-chōme, Arakawa Ward, while the eastern end is in Tsuijidō 2-chōme, Sumida Ward. The bridge's name originates from the nearby {{ill|Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|ja|隅田川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|1=Sumidagawa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658854}}, also known as Suijin-gū.
The bridge was first constructed in March 1989 (Heisei 1). Its assembly was carried out on land, and the bridge girders were transported on barges to the site, where tidal changes were used to place them on the piers.
Nearby parks, {{ill|Shioiri Park|ja|汐入公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioiri Park|1=Shioiri Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11550497}} and {{ill|Higashi Shirahige Park|ja|東白鬚公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Shirahige Park|1=Higashi-Shirahige Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21019411}}, serve as disaster response bases for Tokyo. As part of the disaster preparedness plan, Suijin Ōhashi was built alongside {{ill|Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|1=Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11405350}} to connect these parks. Initially a pedestrian-only bridge, it was opened to vehicular traffic on July 10, 1996 (Heisei 8), following the completion of the connecting roads.
On its eastern side, Suijin Ōhashi connects to the [[:en:Mukojima Route|Shuto Expressway Mukōjima Line]] at the {{ill|Tsuijidō Entrance|ja|堤通出入口|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuijidō Entrance|qq=}}.
This location was historically home to the ferry crossing "{{ill|Suijin no Watashi|ja|水神の渡し|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suijin no Watashi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}."

This location was historically home to the ferry crossing "{{ill|Suijin no Watashi|ja|水神の渡し|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suijin no Watashi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}."

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Suijin Great Bridge]][[ja:水神大橋]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11549046]] Wikidata name: Suijin Ōhashi Bridge [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=July 2024}} {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=461|align=left}} [[File:Suijin ohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View from Sumida Ward, Tokyo (January 2008)]] [[File:Midukamioohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View of Suijin Ōhashi from the Shuto Expressway (August 2005)]] [[File:100 views edo 037.jpg|thumb|200px|Suijin no Watashi]]
'''Suijin Ōhashi''' (水神大橋) is a bridge spanning the [[:en:Sumida River|Sumida River]], carrying a spur of the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Route 461|ja|東京都道461号吾妻橋伊興町線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Metropolitan Route 461|1=Tokyo Metropolitan Road Route 461|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525822}}. The western end of the bridge is located in {{ill|Minami-Senju|ja|南千住|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minami-Senju|1=Minami-Senju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11407548}} 8-chōme, Arakawa Ward, while the eastern end is in Tsuijidō 2-chōme, Sumida Ward. The bridge's name originates from the nearby {{ill|Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|ja|隅田川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|1=Sumidagawa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658854}}, also known as Suijin-gū.
The bridge was first constructed in March 1989 (Heisei 1). Its assembly was carried out on land, and the bridge girders were transported on barges to the site, where tidal changes were used to place them on the piers.
Nearby parks, {{ill|Shioiri Park|ja|汐入公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioiri Park|1=Shioiri Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11550497}} and {{ill|Higashi Shirahige Park|ja|東白鬚公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Shirahige Park|1=Higashi-Shirahige Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21019411}}, serve as disaster response bases for Tokyo. As part of the disaster preparedness plan, Suijin Ōhashi was built alongside {{ill|Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|1=Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11405350}} to connect these parks. Initially a pedestrian-only bridge, it was opened to vehicular traffic on July 10, 1996 (Heisei 8), following the completion of the connecting roads.
On its eastern side, Suijin Ōhashi connects to the [[:en:Mukojima Route|Shuto Expressway Mukōjima Line]] at the {{ill|Tsuijidō Entrance|ja|堤通出入口|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuijidō Entrance|qq=}}.
This location was historically home to the ferry crossing "{{ill|Suijin no Watashi|ja|水神の渡し|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suijin no Watashi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}."
== Bridge Specifications == * '''Type''': Steel road bridge * '''Structure''': Three-span continuous Nielsen-Lohse girder arch bridge * '''Length''': 157.0 meters * '''Main span''': 102.0 meters * '''Width''': 17.0 meters * '''Construction started''': 1984 (Shōwa 59) * '''Completed''': March 1989 (Heisei 1) * '''Project owner''': Tokyo Metropolitan Government * '''Design''': Tokyo Engineering * '''Girder fabrication''': [[:en:Mitsubishi Heavy Industries|Mitsubishi Heavy Industries]]
== Public Transportation == * '''[[:en:Toei Bus|Toei Bus]]''' ** {{ill|Route 40|ja|都営バス南千住営業所#錦40系統|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Route 40|1=Toei Bus Minami-Senju Office|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11643502}}: [[:en:Minami-Senju Station|Minami-Senju Station]] East Exit - Sumida 2-chōme - Shirahige Bridge - Higashi Mukōjima Hirokōji - [[:en:Tokyo Skytree Station|Tokyo Skytree Station]] Front - [[:en:Kinshichō Station|Kinshichō Station]] Front

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Suijin Great Bridge]][[ja:水神大橋]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Suijin Great Bridge]][[ja:水神大橋]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11549046]] Wikidata name: Suijin Ōhashi Bridge [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=July 2024}} {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=461|align=left}} [[File:Suijin ohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View from Sumida Ward, Tokyo (January 2008)]] [[File:Midukamioohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View of Suijin Ōhashi from the Shuto Expressway (August 2005)]] [[File:100 views edo 037.jpg|thumb|200px|Suijin no Watashi]]
'''Suijin Ōhashi''' (水神大橋) is a bridge spanning the [[:en:Sumida River|Sumida River]], carrying a spur of the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Route 461|ja|東京都道461号吾妻橋伊興町線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Metropolitan Route 461|1=Tokyo Metropolitan Road Route 461|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525822}}. The western end of the bridge is located in {{ill|Minami-Senju|ja|南千住|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minami-Senju|1=Minami-Senju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11407548}} 8-chōme, Arakawa Ward, while the eastern end is in Tsuijidō 2-chōme, Sumida Ward. The bridge's name originates from the nearby {{ill|Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|ja|隅田川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|1=Sumidagawa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658854}}, also known as Suijin-gū.
The bridge was first constructed in March 1989 (Heisei 1). Its assembly was carried out on land, and the bridge girders were transported on barges to the site, where tidal changes were used to place them on the piers.
Nearby parks, {{ill|Shioiri Park|ja|汐入公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioiri Park|1=Shioiri Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11550497}} and {{ill|Higashi Shirahige Park|ja|東白鬚公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Shirahige Park|1=Higashi-Shirahige Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21019411}}, serve as disaster response bases for Tokyo. As part of the disaster preparedness plan, Suijin Ōhashi was built alongside {{ill|Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|1=Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11405350}} to connect these parks. Initially a pedestrian-only bridge, it was opened to vehicular traffic on July 10, 1996 (Heisei 8), following the completion of the connecting roads.
On its eastern side, Suijin Ōhashi connects to the [[:en:Mukojima Route|Shuto Expressway Mukōjima Line]] at the {{ill|Tsuijidō Entrance|ja|堤通出入口|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuijidō Entrance|qq=}}.
This location was historically home to the ferry crossing "{{ill|Suijin no Watashi|ja|水神の渡し|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suijin no Watashi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}."

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Suijin Great Bridge]][[ja:水神大橋]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Suijin Great Bridge]][[ja:水神大橋]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Suijin Great Bridge]][[ja:水神大橋]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11549046]] Wikidata name: Suijin Ōhashi Bridge [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=July 2024}} {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=461|align=left}} [[File:Suijin ohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View from Sumida Ward, Tokyo (January 2008)]] [[File:Midukamioohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View of Suijin Ōhashi from the Shuto Expressway (August 2005)]] [[File:100 views edo 037.jpg|thumb|200px|Suijin no Watashi]]
'''Suijin Ōhashi''' (水神大橋) is a bridge spanning the [[:en:Sumida River|Sumida River]], carrying a spur of the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Route 461|ja|東京都道461号吾妻橋伊興町線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Metropolitan Route 461|1=Tokyo Metropolitan Road Route 461|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525822}}. The western end of the bridge is located in {{ill|Minami-Senju|ja|南千住|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minami-Senju|1=Minami-Senju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11407548}} 8-chōme, Arakawa Ward, while the eastern end is in Tsuijidō 2-chōme, Sumida Ward. The bridge's name originates from the nearby {{ill|Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|ja|隅田川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|1=Sumidagawa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658854}}, also known as Suijin-gū.
The bridge was first constructed in March 1989 (Heisei 1). Its assembly was carried out on land, and the bridge girders were transported on barges to the site, where tidal changes were used to place them on the piers.
Nearby parks, {{ill|Shioiri Park|ja|汐入公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioiri Park|1=Shioiri Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11550497}} and {{ill|Higashi Shirahige Park|ja|東白鬚公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Shirahige Park|1=Higashi-Shirahige Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21019411}}, serve as disaster response bases for Tokyo. As part of the disaster preparedness plan, Suijin Ōhashi was built alongside {{ill|Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|1=Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11405350}} to connect these parks. Initially a pedestrian-only bridge, it was opened to vehicular traffic on July 10, 1996 (Heisei 8), following the completion of the connecting roads.
On its eastern side, Suijin Ōhashi connects to the [[:en:Mukojima Route|Shuto Expressway Mukōjima Line]] at the {{ill|Tsuijidō Entrance|ja|堤通出入口|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuijidō Entrance|qq=}}.
This location was historically home to the ferry crossing "{{ill|Suijin no Watashi|ja|水神の渡し|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suijin no Watashi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}."

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Suijin Great Bridge]][[ja:水神大橋]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Suijin Great Bridge]][[ja:水神大橋]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Suijin Great Bridge]][[ja:水神大橋]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11549046]] Wikidata name: Suijin Ōhashi Bridge [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=July 2024}} {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=461|align=left}} [[File:Suijin ohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View from Sumida Ward, Tokyo (January 2008)]] [[File:Midukamioohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View of Suijin Ōhashi from the Shuto Expressway (August 2005)]] [[File:100 views edo 037.jpg|thumb|200px|Suijin no Watashi]]
'''Suijin Ōhashi''' (水神大橋) is a bridge spanning the [[:en:Sumida River|Sumida River]], carrying a spur of the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Route 461|ja|東京都道461号吾妻橋伊興町線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Metropolitan Route 461|1=Tokyo Metropolitan Road Route 461|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525822}}. The western end of the bridge is located in {{ill|Minami-Senju|ja|南千住|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minami-Senju|1=Minami-Senju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11407548}} 8-chōme, Arakawa Ward, while the eastern end is in Tsuijidō 2-chōme, Sumida Ward. The bridge's name originates from the nearby {{ill|Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|ja|隅田川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|1=Sumidagawa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658854}}, also known as Suijin-gū.
The bridge was first constructed in March 1989 (Heisei 1). Its assembly was carried out on land, and the bridge girders were transported on barges to the site, where tidal changes were used to place them on the piers.
Nearby parks, {{ill|Shioiri Park|ja|汐入公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioiri Park|1=Shioiri Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11550497}} and {{ill|Higashi Shirahige Park|ja|東白鬚公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Shirahige Park|1=Higashi-Shirahige Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21019411}}, serve as disaster response bases for Tokyo. As part of the disaster preparedness plan, Suijin Ōhashi was built alongside {{ill|Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|1=Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11405350}} to connect these parks. Initially a pedestrian-only bridge, it was opened to vehicular traffic on July 10, 1996 (Heisei 8), following the completion of the connecting roads.
On its eastern side, Suijin Ōhashi connects to the [[:en:Mukojima Route|Shuto Expressway Mukōjima Line]] at the {{ill|Tsuijidō Entrance|ja|堤通出入口|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuijidō Entrance|qq=}}.
This location was historically home to the ferry crossing "{{ill|Suijin no Watashi|ja|水神の渡し|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suijin no Watashi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}."

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Suijin Great Bridge]][[ja:水神大橋]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Suijin Great Bridge]][[ja:水神大橋]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Suijin Great Bridge]][[ja:水神大橋]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11549046]] Wikidata name: Suijin Ōhashi Bridge [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=July 2024}} {{Ja_Pref_Route_Sign|pref=Tokyo|number=461|align=left}} [[File:Suijin ohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View from Sumida Ward, Tokyo (January 2008)]] [[File:Midukamioohashi.jpg|thumb|200px|View of Suijin Ōhashi from the Shuto Expressway (August 2005)]] [[File:100 views edo 037.jpg|thumb|200px|Suijin no Watashi]]
'''Suijin Ōhashi''' (水神大橋) is a bridge spanning the {{ill|Sumida River|en|Sumida River|WD=Q222149|lt=Sumida River}}, carrying a spur of the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Route 461|ja|東京都道461号吾妻橋伊興町線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Metropolitan Route 461|1=Tokyo Metropolitan Road Route 461|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525822}}. The western end of the bridge is located in {{ill|Minami-Senju|ja|南千住|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minami-Senju|1=Minami-Senju|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11407548}} 8-chōme, Arakawa Ward, while the eastern end is in Tsuijidō 2-chōme, Sumida Ward. The bridge's name originates from the nearby {{ill|Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|ja|隅田川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|1=Sumidagawa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658854}}, also known as Suijin-gū.
The bridge was first constructed in March 1989 (Heisei 1). Its assembly was carried out on land, and the bridge girders were transported on barges to the site, where tidal changes were used to place them on the piers.
Nearby parks, {{ill|Shioiri Park|ja|汐入公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioiri Park|1=Shioiri Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11550497}} and {{ill|Higashi Shirahige Park|ja|東白鬚公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Shirahige Park|1=Higashi-Shirahige Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21019411}}, serve as disaster response bases for Tokyo. As part of the disaster preparedness plan, Suijin Ōhashi was built alongside {{ill|Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|1=Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11405350}} to connect these parks. Initially a pedestrian-only bridge, it was opened to vehicular traffic on July 10, 1996 (Heisei 8), following the completion of the connecting roads.
On its eastern side, Suijin Ōhashi connects to the {{ill|Mukojima Route|en|Mukojima Route|WD=Q11667098|lt=Shuto Expressway Mukōjima Line}} at the {{ill|Tsuijidō Entrance|ja|堤通出入口|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuijidō Entrance|qq=}}.
This location was historically home to the ferry crossing "{{ill|Suijin no Watashi|ja|水神の渡し|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suijin no Watashi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}."
== Bridge Specifications == * '''Type''': Steel road bridge * '''Structure''': Three-span continuous Nielsen-Lohse girder arch bridge * '''Length''': 157.0 meters * '''Main span''': 102.0 meters * '''Width''': 17.0 meters * '''Construction started''': 1984 (Shōwa 59) * '''Completed''': March 1989 (Heisei 1) * '''Project owner''': Tokyo Metropolitan Government * '''Design''': Tokyo Engineering * '''Girder fabrication''': {{ill|Mitsubishi Heavy Industries|en|Mitsubishi Heavy Industries|WD=Q648280|lt=Mitsubishi Heavy Industries}}
== Public Transportation == * '''{{ill|Toei Bus|en|Toei Bus|WD=Q859227|lt=Toei Bus}}''' ** {{ill|Route 40|ja|都営バス南千住営業所#錦40系統|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Route 40|1=Toei Bus Minami-Senju Office|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11643502}}: {{ill|Minami-Senju Station|en|Minami-Senju Station|WD=Q80570|lt=Minami-Senju Station}} East Exit - Sumida 2-chōme - Shirahige Bridge - Higashi Mukōjima Hirokōji - {{ill|Tokyo Skytree Station|en|Tokyo Skytree Station|WD=Q900250|lt=Tokyo Skytree Station}} Front - {{ill|Kinshichō Station|en|Kinshichō Station|WD=Q801017|lt=Kinshichō Station}} Front
== Nearby Bridges == * {{ill|Sumida River|en|Sumida River|WD=Q222149|lt=Sumida River}} : (Upstream) - {{ill|Sumida River Bridge (Tokyo Metro Hibiya Line)|ja|隅田川橋梁 (東京地下鉄日比谷線)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumida River Bridge (Tokyo Metro Hibiya Line)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} - {{ill|Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|1=Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11405350}} * '''Suijin Ōhashi''' - {{ill|Shirahige Bridge|ja|白鬚橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirahige Bridge|qq=}} - {{ill|Sakura Bridge (Tokyo)|ja|桜橋 (東京都)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakura Bridge (Tokyo)|1=Sakura Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538116}} - (Downstream)

The bridge's name originates from the nearby {{ill|Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|ja|隅田川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumidagawa Shrine (Suijin-gū)|1=Sumidagawa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11658854}}, also known as Suijin-gū.
The bridge was first constructed in March 1989 (Heisei 1). Its assembly was carried out on land, and the bridge girders were transported on barges to the site, where tidal changes were used to place them on the piers.
Nearby parks, {{ill|Shioiri Park|ja|汐入公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shioiri Park|1=Shioiri Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11550497}} and {{ill|Higashi Shirahige Park|ja|東白鬚公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Shirahige Park|1=Higashi-Shirahige Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21019411}}, serve as disaster response bases for Tokyo. As part of the disaster preparedness plan, Suijin Ōhashi was built alongside {{ill|Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|1=Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11405350}} to connect these parks. Initially a pedestrian-only bridge, it was opened to vehicular traffic on July 10, 1996 (Heisei 8), following the completion of the connecting roads.
On its eastern side, Suijin Ōhashi connects to the {{ill|Mukojima Route|en|Mukojima Route|WD=Q11667098|lt=Shuto Expressway Mukōjima Line|1=Mukojima Route}} at the {{ill|Tsuijidō Entrance|ja|堤通出入口|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuijidō Entrance|qq=}}.
This location was historically home to the ferry crossing "{{ill|Suijin no Watashi|ja|水神の渡し|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suijin no Watashi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}."
== Bridge Specifications == * '''Type''': Steel road bridge * '''Structure''': Three-span continuous Nielsen-Lohse girder arch bridge * '''Length''': 157.0 meters * '''Main span''': 102.0 meters * '''Width''': 17.0 meters * '''Construction started''': 1984 (Shōwa 59) * '''Completed''': March 1989 (Heisei 1) * '''Project owner''': Tokyo Metropolitan Government * '''Design''': Tokyo Engineering * '''Girder fabrication''': {{ill|Mitsubishi Heavy Industries|en|Mitsubishi Heavy Industries|WD=Q648280|lt=Mitsubishi Heavy Industries|1=Mitsubishi Heavy Industries}}
== Public Transportation == * '''{{ill|Toei Bus|en|Toei Bus|WD=Q859227|lt=Toei Bus|1=Toei Bus}}''' ** {{ill|Route 40|ja|都営バス南千住営業所#錦40系統|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Route 40|1=Toei Bus Minami-Senju Office|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11643502}}: {{ill|Minami-Senju Station|en|Minami-Senju Station|WD=Q80570|lt=Minami-Senju Station|1=Minami-Senju Station}} East Exit - Sumida 2-chōme - Shirahige Bridge - Higashi Mukōjima Hirokōji - {{ill|Tokyo Skytree Station|en|Tokyo Skytree Station|WD=Q900250|lt=Tokyo Skytree Station|1=Tokyo Skytree Station}} Front - {{ill|Kinshichō Station|en|Kinshichō Station|WD=Q801017|lt=Kinshichō Station|1=Kinshichō Station}} Front
== Nearby Bridges == * {{ill|Sumida River|en|Sumida River|WD=Q222149|lt=Sumida River|1=Sumida River}} : (Upstream) - {{ill|Sumida River Bridge (Tokyo Metro Hibiya Line)|ja|隅田川橋梁 (東京地下鉄日比谷線)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumida River Bridge (Tokyo Metro Hibiya Line)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} - {{ill|Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|ja|千住汐入大橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|1=Senju Shioiri Ōhashi Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11405350}} * '''Suijin Ōhashi''' - {{ill|Shirahige Bridge|ja|白鬚橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirahige Bridge|qq=}} - {{ill|Sakura Bridge (Tokyo)|ja|桜橋 (東京都)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakura Bridge (Tokyo)|1=Sakura Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11538116}} - (Downstream)
